system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task C - Health app data privacy policy

## Brief

A direct-to-consumer health app (~2M monthly active users) is launching an AI-powered health coaching feature. The product team must choose a primary data policy that governs how user health data is processed by the AI. The choice has implications for capability, privacy, regulatory exposure, cost, and trust. The CEO asks for a primary recommendation.

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Local-only processing. All AI inference happens on the user's device. No health data leaves the phone. The model is smaller (capability ceiling 60-70% of cloud-equivalent) but privacy is structurally maximal.

**Option B:** Encrypted cloud with no training. User health data sent to the cloud encrypted, decrypted only at inference time in a confidential-computing enclave, never used to train any model. Full capability, ~3x infrastructure cost.

**Option C:** Cloud with opt-out training. Data sent to the cloud, used for personalization by default, used for model improvement unless user opts out. Full capability, standard cost, regulatory grey area in some jurisdictions.

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).

**Option E:** Federated learning. Personalization happens locally; aggregate model updates sent to cloud, individual data does not leave the device. Privacy preserved at architectural level. Capability and reliability still under active research; deployment complexity is high.

## Task for each agent

Recommend the most appropriate policy and explain why. 200-400 words. Advocate a position rather than enumerating all options.

## Why this task

- Different domain from A (capital allocation) and B (organizational diagnosis): product ethics with regulatory and technical dimensions.
- Multi-axis tradeoffs: privacy / capability / cost / trust / complexity.
- No clear right answer; reasonable agents will weight axes differently.
- 5 elements suitable for triadic procedure.
- Cross-validates whether method works on a third domain not seen in pilot.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
# Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option B is the right primary policy.**

The core argument is that health data is uniquely sensitive, and the policy choice will define the product's trust posture for years. The other options each fail on a critical axis:

- **Option A** sacrifices too much capability. A health coach operating at 60-70% of cloud-equivalent quality risks giving worse advice on matters that affect real health outcomes. "Maximally private but mediocre" is the wrong tradeoff when the product's value proposition is competent coaching.
- **Option C** is a regulatory and reputational time bomb. Default opt-out training on health data is precisely the pattern that triggers GDPR Article 9 enforcement, state-level health privacy laws (Washington's MHMDA, etc.), and the kind of news cycle that destroys consumer health brands.
- **Option D** is defensible but still couples the product's data pipeline to training ambitions, creating ongoing consent-management complexity and a default-on flow that regulators increasingly scrutinize as dark-pattern-adjacent.
- **Option E** is architecturally elegant but operationally premature. "Capability and reliability still under active research" is not a foundation for a launched product serving 2M users.

**Option B aligns incentives cleanly.** Separating *use of data for the user* from *use of data for the company* eliminates the central conflict of interest in health AI. Users get full-capability coaching; the company gets a defensible, marketable privacy story ("your data is never used to train our models, ever"); regulators get a clean architecture to audit; the legal team gets a much smaller surface area.

The 3x infrastructure cost is the real objection. But: (a) inference cost per active coaching user is likely a small fraction of total CAC and support cost in a consumer health app; (b) confidential computing premiums are falling rapidly; (c) the cost buys both regulatory insulation and a genuine competitive differentiator in a category where trust is the binding constraint on growth.

Model improvement can still happen via explicit, separately-consented research programs, synthetic data, or licensed datasets — decoupled from the production data path.

**Recommend Option B as primary policy, with a roadmap to evaluate Option E as the technology matures.**

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).
